item.title: Warhammer 40,000: Mechanicus II Review
item.content:encoded:
Despite providing an iconic grimdark setting, one that ought to be perfect video game fodder, the Warhammer 40,000 license has often been something of a poisoned chalice for developers. It’s really only in the last decade, with the likes of Space Marine 2, the significantly patched Darktide and Rogue Trader arriving on the scene, that the licence has finally been given the digital respect it deserves. The original Warhammer 40,000 Mechanicus, released back in 2018, was an almost very good turn-based strategy game that pitted the deeply unsexy cyborgs of the Adeptus Mecahnicus against the unrelenting hordes of the robo-zombie-pharaohs, the Necrons. With the sequel now released, have developers Bulwork Studios delivered on the XCOM-in-40K promise of the original?

First off, if you are a fan of the multitude of Warhammer books released by the Black Library, then you are going to love the ludicrously excellent storyline that accompanies Mechanicus 2. With veteran author Ben Counter on scriptwriting duties, you know you’re in safe hands. Plus, the fully voiced dialogue brings the characters to distinctive 40K life. Overall, this is a deeply satisfying romp, packed with vast quantities of fan service to enjoy, and is the type of compelling narrative that will keep you playing through what is sadly a rather lacklustre strategy game.

Things start off promisingly enough in a neat prologue that switches between the player taking control of the Adeptus Mechanicus and Necron forces. It’s a great setup, effortlessly introducing the key protagonists and the main strategic differences between the rival armies. After the intro, you’re free to pick your side and dive into the game proper. It’s here that the game takes a sudden downward drop. A good strategy game is all about choice: the player is flooded with a range of tactical options – ideally with numerous solutions for any given combat scenario – and must correctly differentiate the correct approach. It’s this that gives the player agency, providing them with the opportunity to experiment and then rewarding them with a feeling of satisfaction when they achieve their goals. This is not the case for Mechanicus 2, which is unrelentingly, overwhelmingly, linear.

Despite the presence of a global map, you are not free to engage the enemy wherever you wish and you must instead complete missions in a strictly linear order. Then, once within a mission, the unit you have selected follows a prescribed path, encountering enemy forces on their way, in a manner reminiscent of an old JRPG. You can’t attempt to avoid or flank an enemy, you just bump into an invisible opponent and suddenly fight. There are some tactical choices to be made during this section, in which you must choose between a variety of text-based options, but the solution is usually so blindingly obvious the entire mechanic is rendered pointless. If in doubt, choose to be cautious every time.

Once into the turn-based battles themselves, things improve, but not by as much as you would hope. Yes, there are many differences between the units that comprise the Necrons and the Adeptus Mechanicus, but they rarely translate into meaningfully distinct gameplay. The structure of the combat doesn’t help here either, with control – as in the original – passing back and forth between player and AI after each individual unit’s turn is complete. This proves unsatisfying, as it means it’s impossible to move your force as one to deftly unleash some clever flanking manoeuvre or defensive stratagem. Instead, combat proves bitty and piecemeal, with victory achieved through attrition rather than skill. I mean, it’s reliably fine turn-based fare, but soon becomes repetitive due to a lack of player agency.

On top of all this are numerous technical issues on PS5 – including one where the entire game needs to often be rebooted as the controller stops working – and painful loading times and you have a disappointing sequel that doesn’t come close to improving on the original game.

Summary

What should have been a confident stride forwards from the solid tactical action of the original instead feels like a nervous sidestep. The superb narrative of Warhammer 40,000: Mechanicus 2, bolstered by strong voiceovers and rich fan service, gives players every reason to be invested in its grimdark world, but that investment is steadily undermined by overly linear gameplay and combat that rarely rises above the mundane.

Good

  • A great 40K story, well told
  • You can play as the Necrons this time
  • Turn-based combat is solid

Bad

  • Overly linear campaign progression
  • Limited tactical options
  • It's often a bit boring

Related stories from TSA



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/06/01/warhammer-40000-mechanicus-ii-review/

item.title: CAPCOM May Have Uphill Battle With Onimusha: Way of the Sword, Targeting September Against Marvel’s Wolverine, And 2 Heavy Hitters
item.content:encoded:
• 0-20%: Unlikely - Lacks credible sources
  • 21-40%: Questionable - Some concerns remain
  • 41-60%: Plausible - Reasonable evidence
  • 61-80%: Probable - Strong evidence
  • 81-100%: Highly Likely - Multiple reliable sources

With Grand Theft Auto 6 confirmed for a November 19 release, most major publishers are doing everything they can to launch their games before Rockstar Games releases what is expected to be a record-breaking game. As a result, September 2026 is looking to be the most packed month in years, but some games may have an uphill battle nonetheless, such as CAPCOM's Onimusha: Way of the Sword, the first new entry in the series in a very long time.

Over the weekend, X user @intercelluar, who proved their credibility by leaking footage of Assassin's Creed: Black Flag Resynced, and more recently, revealing the yet-to-be-officially confirmed Rayman Legends Retold, which has been corroborated by multiple sources, revealed that Onimusha: Way of the Sword is supposedly launching on September 25. This, however, is old info, and may be outdated.

While the game may not release exactly on September 25, chances are still very high for a September release window. "Was only ever told a Sept release target; but never a specific date. I had heard September window a fair bit ago," said reliable insider NateDrake on the ResetERA forums. "Late Sept makes a lot of sense for release. I don't know if the exact day is correct; but the month of September at least tracks with the release timing expectation," the insider continued.

Although there's little doubt about the game's quality, considering the quality of CAPCOM's recent games like Resident Evil Requiem and PRAGMATA, Onimusha: Way of the Sword may still face intense release window congestion if it indeed launches this September. The month is going to be packed with games targeting similar audiences, starting with Phantom Blade Zero launching on September 9, followed by Marvel's Wolverine launching on September 15. In addition, Gears of War: E-Day is reportedly also targeting a September launch.

Hopefully, the game's unique mechanics, which I had the chance to test at last year's Gamescom, will be enough to ensure the return of the long-dormant series is successful.

About the author: Francesco De Meo has been covering video games and technology since 2012, starting his career at small outlets like Gamersyndrome and GeekSnack. After joining Wccftech gaming section in 2015, he quickly expanded his video gaming coverage with in-depth reporting, interviews with iconic industry figures such as Grasshopper Manufacture founder and No More Heroes creator Goichi "Suda51" Suda, Resident Evil series creator Shinji Mikami, Team NINJA's president and Nioh series director Fumihiko Yasuda, and Silent Hill creator Keiichiro Toyama, reviews and on-the-ground coverage of major industry events such as Gamescom and E3. When he's not reporting or reviewing, Francesco can be found playing the genres he loves most, spending time with his six cats, reading, writing music, playing guitar and drumming for his progressive rock band.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/capcom-onimusha-way-of-the-sword-targeting-september/

item.title: Fable's delay into 2027 is the right move, but it'll make looking through the window bars at GTA 6 on consoles slightly more painful
item.content:encoded:
There ain't enough room for two morally dubious knobbing simulators this autumn, Xbox's Booty reckons

Ah, the Fable reboot isn't coming out in 2026 after all, then. Rumours had suggested the wind was blowing in that direction and Xbox exec Matt Booty chose to interrupt last Friday's chill vibes to confirm that a delay to February 2027's now been set in stone, ripping the plaster off ahead of the impending Summer Geoff Fest. While almost certainly a shrewd move on Playground Games' part, Fable's slip from the autumn slate still sucks if you're, say, on the platform this November's flashiest arrival isn't hitting yet.

Yep, won't someone think of us poor PC players, I lamented as I came into work this fine morning and went about dissecting what Fable's latest slip means in practical terms. The short answer is that one potential distraction from the inevitable wall of noise around GTA 6's console release on November 19th is now gone. While making sure to name-drop all of the Xbox adjacent games which have been or will be arriving this year, Booty's delay announcement via the Xbox podcast basically made no secret of which game Fable's dodging with this move.

"We talked about Forza Horizon 6 and how the team at Playground is doing really well," he said. "Also doing really well on Fable. The game is in great shape. The team feels really good. We're excited about where the game is. We want to make sure that that game has a window all to its own, so we are going to move it from this fall to February. And again, we really just want it to have its own moment to shine."

We know this stings, but it won’t be much longer before you’re back in Albion making morally questionable decisions.

We’ll be sharing some big Fable reveals @XBOX Showcase on June 7th, so be sure to tune in.#Fable #FableTheGame https://t.co/eyEQOGCn4w

— Fable (@fable) May 29, 2026

To see this content please enable targeting cookies.

The timing, especially with Booty confirming more of Fable will be shown at Xbox's summer showcase on June 7th, very much smacks of waiting to make sure GTA 6 didn't suffer another delay this spring, then putting plans in place to slot in after it. Given the amount of console oxygen GTA 6 will take up, it's a no brainer. It'd be even more of a no brainer if Rockstar's crime sim was coming to PC at launch as well, but we can't have that, can we? Take-Two boss Strauss Zelnick and co are determined to make folks on our side of the fence wait.

Given that, it might've been cool to see whether Fable could've made at least some fight of it in autumn with its PC presence as a key weapon working in its favour. On the other hand, those are the sorts of bets companies understandably won't fancy making, not to mention the fact Playground Games' RPG will also haemorrhage some potential sales due to the BDS movement's ongoing boycott of parent company Microsoft over the tech giant's ties to the Israeli military.

So, early next year it is, and us PC players will have to look for other games to be digging into while GTA 6 arrives on the other game boxes. As much as Grand Theft feels like it's scrambled this year's release schedule - see the fact about 500 noteworthy games (including some we simply lacked the capacity to take on editorially) came out in May to give themselves a nice summer runway - there'll inevitably still be cool new stuff to try once the leaves start to turn brown. Maybe I'll have to find a way to make my initial Blood of Dawnwalker playthrough last about three to four months.



https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/fables-delay-into-2027-is-the-right-move-but-itll-make-looking-through-the-window-bars-at-gta-6-on-consoles-slightly-more-painful

item.title: Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon Review - Space Marines Vs Orks In All-Out Combat
item.content:encoded:
A new edition of Warhammer 40,000 has been released, and as always, the new edition launches with an explosive new box featuring the story of Operation Imperator and miniatures from the Space Marines and Orks that will wage war against one another.

What's In The Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon Box?

Inside Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon, you'll find the following:

  • Armageddon: Operation Imperator book detailing the lore of the campaign and the two enemy factions (Exclusive to this box set)
  • 61 different miniatures comprising both the Space Marine and Ork armies, these miniatures are push-fit for new players' ease (and honestly, returning players too)
    • 23 miniatures for the Space Marines
    • 38 miniatures for the Orks
  • An assembly book with instructions on how to assemble the two armies
  • The Core Rules booklet to teach you the game, at a size to make it readily available
  • Dominatus Narrative Campaign Deck
  • Chapter Approved 2026-2027 Mission Deck
  • 17 Datasheet cards for each of the unit types included in the box
  • Transfer sheets for model icons and symbols

This box will essentially feature everything a returning or new player will need to start an Armageddon campaign. Inside this tome, you'll get caught up on the lore from Armageddon: The Return of Yarrick, and it will give you information about the different chapters of Space Marines and Ork clans that are fighting across this desolate planet.

This 114-page guide will give you everything you need to understand the greater story of the latest assault on Armageddon across the various theaters of war.

The Core Rulebook is a small-scale book, easy to tuck to the side or pass around, filled with the rules of the game. This Core Rules booklet takes a well-structured approach to teaching the game. First, make sure a player has the foundation of understanding their Datasheets, basic terms, and keywords. From there, it builds upon a battle from step 1 as you're setting up your pieces and then through each of the phases of the Battle Round.

With detailed examples of movement and combat between multiple factions and unit types the rules efficiently teach the player how quickly combat can get complicated, but how easy it can also be to resolve.

Working through the Core Rules once with a keen eye should set you up to really put it away and just focus on playing the game. Frontloading the learning experience for a better experience overall.

Assemble Your Armies

This box has all you need to build two armies. All of the miniatures are push-fit, while you might need to cut out each of the pieces you won't need any additional tools to put everything together.

There are 12 sprue sheets and an Assembly Guide that clearly outlines which sheet you should be looking at, as well as highlighting which of the parts from the sprue sheet are needed to put together your units.

The guides also do a good job of highlighting where there are multiple pieces that you can use at a certain time. For the most part, this comes down to whether your Space Marines are wearing their helmets or not. Alternatively, the Ork have color-coded instructions depending on the pose or armaments you want them equipped with.

The Blue built Boyz might have an assault rifle, green could have an axe and a handgun, and pink might have a gun and a hand grenade. There's a lot of fun opportunity here to outfit your armies in a way that best fits your gameplay aesthetic as well as playstyle preference.

Chapter Approved and Dominatus Decks

Getting into the game, you and your opponent will use the Chapter Approved decks to create a well-balanced and thematic game of Warhammer.

It starts with each player selecting their army and picking a Force Disposition Card; these cards have one of five symbols on them representing different activity types. Priority Assets, Reconnaissance, Disruption, Purge The Foe, and Take and Hold. Once both players have selected a card, they're both shown, comparing the two symbols, each player will take a mission that will be their way of earning Victory Points.

For example, Player 1 selecting Purge the Foe and Player 2 selecting Priority Assets will mean Player 1 gets the mission Destroyer's Wrath, earning them Victory Points in rounds where one or more enemy units were destroyed, if you're controlling an objective, or you killed more units in a turn than were killed by you. Player 2's mission is Sabotage, which gives you access to the special Sabotage action. You'll earn Victory Points when you commit sabotage, especially if the unit is within range of an objective or you control an objective.

While you and your opponent are still at each other's throats, these Force Disposition Cards allow you to play the game in a style that fits you better. That's not to say there won't be some matchups that might not work out in your favor, but then you'll just have to make sure you're really playing strategically.

From there, you then set up deployment, determine if you want to add a gameplay twist by adding an additional card, and then set up the rest of your battlefield and start your battle. What will determine the winner is how many Victory Points you have at the end of combat.

Where the Chapter Approved deck is good for setting up a battle for the sake of a battle by using the Dominatus Deck, you'll instead get a narrative experience.

These battles are played out over three Phases. With each phase, you'll have a Liberator (Normally Imperium) and Opressor (Orks) alliance. You'll roll to pick a different location for the phase, and in a similar manner to Chapter Approved battles will pick your Force Dispositions.

Depending on what is selected, an Agenda is selected. For a battle where the Liberators have selected the Skull and the Opressors have selected the blade, the Liberator's focus will be on the Vital Strongpoint Agenda, and your opponent will have the Repel The Foe.

Again, each army will have its own set of objectives to obtain Victory Points, and a way to achieve its Agenda, but they'll be more narratively linked. The example above sets up an almost 'king of the hill' narrative to the story where the Oppressors are trying to retain control of their Objectives.

I like the idea of allowing people to have their choice of a slightly thematic 'pick up' game where they can get into the battle fast with unique objectives to keep every game fresh, even when playing the same armies, but then if you're going to really spend some time playing there's an opportunity to build out a story and higher stakes along with a steadily upgrading force to enhance replayability and to keep things fresh.

Adeptus Astartes Units In Action

The Space Marines included in Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon are:

  • Captain
  • Librarian
  • Chaplain with Jump Pack
  • Ancient
  • 10x Intercessors
  • 3x Eradicators with Heavy Bolters
  • 5x Vanguard Veterans
  • Land Speeder

As you'd expect of the Space Marines, this faction is versatile and has plenty of options between ranged and melee combat. Some unique abilities of the Leader and Support units of this faction are Captain With Relic Shield's Finest Hour. This allows you, once per battle, to give this model's melee weapon +3 A and [Devastating Wounds].

The Chaplain with Jump Pack has Litany of Hate, which gives you +1 to wound rolls, and Exhortation of Rage that lets you have a chance to inflict mortal wounds on a target you're engaged with. The Librarion has Feel No Pain 4+ against psychic attacks, along with a 4+ InSv.

The Ancient has far slower movement, but has +1 OC, and its Unbreakable Resolve ability allows that when the model is within range of an objective or within 6" of the center of the battlefield, it has Feel No Pain 4 +.

The three squads – Intercessor Squad, Vanguard Veteran Squad, and Eradicator Squad – have the respective abilities to secure a controller objective and give +2 A on a bolt rifle attack, gain [Lethal Hits] when making a charge move, and add [Blast 1] to attacks against visible non-Monster/Vehicle enemy units.

The Land Speeder lets you move D6" after making a shot in the Shooting Phase, and depending on how you're feeling, you can either set it up with an onslaught gatling cannon or a heavy flamer.

Orks - Ready For A Big Scrap

The Orks included in Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon are:

  • Warboss
  • Bigboss
  • Bannernob
  • Painboy
  • Weirdboy
  • 20x Boyz
  • 10x Gretchin
  • Wartrakk
  • Big Mek Dakkarig

The Orks are different in that they have a few more big units, but also a lot more fodder to throw out there.

The Warboss gives +1 to melee weapon Hit Rolls, and while the Waaagh! Faction Ability is in effect his melee attacks have a +4 A. Bigboss has [Sustained Hits 1], and +1 to Charge Rolls, Bannernob has +5 InSv, and while Waagh! is active, has +1 T, and Painbow has Feel No Pain 5+ and can inflict D6 mortal wounds when getting a critical hit with its 'urty syringe against a non-vehicle.

The Weirdboy has Waaagh! Energy that for every 5 models in this unit, you add 1 to S and D of the 'Eadbanger weapon, and it can also gain [Hazardous] at 10 or more models. Weirdboy can also make a psychic leap once per turn per army. On a 1, you might take D6 mortal wounds, but on 2-6 you place the unit in strategic reserves, and it gets Deep Strike.

After that, you've got your Boyz and Gretchin, and a vehicle, and a mount. Some smaller units to flood the field and secure objectives, and bigger hitters to really put the pressure on your opponent.

Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon Review | Final Thoughts

There's certainly a lot to unpack here and a great way to get started with a new Warhammer 40,000 release. Returning to a familiar setting with an expanded lore and objectives to play, whether you want just the combat, or combat with a bit of story, this box will get you going in no time.

Snap-fit miniatures work to reduce the barrier to entry with this box and offer a nice level of customization for fans to create the army they want, or for a newcomer to understand what their options are.

Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon was reviewed with a copy provided by Games Workshop - All photos were taken over the course of the review



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/reviews/warhammer-40000-armageddon-review-space-marines-vs-orks-in-all-out-combat

item.title: Tekken 8 game director Kohei Ikeda leaves after 20 years at Bandai Namco, amidst on-going series woes
item.content:encoded:
Kohei Ikeda - director of Tekken 8 and 20-year veteran at Bandai Namco - has announced his departure from the company.

In a statement posted on social media, Ikeda expressed his love for fighting games and his coworkers on the Tekken development team.

"I am deeply proud that I had the opportunity to create games alongside such incredible people who poured their hearts into this work," Ikeda wrote. "While my role leading the team as game director is coming to a close, the values that both I personally and the Tekken project have always cherished - staying close to the fans and the community and building the game together with everyone - will never change.

"I have entrusted those values, and that baton, to the incredible team that will continue to shape the future of Tekken. As I look forward to the future of Tekken and the FGC (fighting game community), I will also continue taking on new challenges as a game developer. I hope our paths cross again someday."

While the statement indicates he's not retiring, Ikeda has not revealed what his next step in his game development career will be. His departure follows Tekken 8 executive game director Katsuhiro Harada in December 2025, as well as producer Yohei Shimbori in August 2025.

This announcement comes at a time of controversy for Tekken 8. While official support for the game has continued, including the recent reveal of Grappler Baki's Yujio Hanma as the game's next DLC character, recent major updates have been received poorly by its fanbase.

The game's third season patch garnered the ire of Tekken 8 fans, who expressed frustration at the game's overly aggressive nature standing firm in spite of developer pledges to bring things "back to basics". While emergency patches were announced in response to this feedback, it marked a continuous air of frustration surrounding the game.

As such the future of Tekken currently is a bit of a mystery. Meanwhile, some of its former leads have started new ventures, including Harada's new studio backed by SNK. Harada, interestingly enough, has commented on the future of Tekken in new hands, believing it won't be hard for the series to "continue with someone else".

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/tekken-8-director-kohei-ikeda-leaves

item.title: NVIDIA Doubles Down on DLSS 4.5 With Smarter Ray Reconstruction at Computex, But DLSS 5 Is a No-Show
item.content:encoded:
At Computex 2026, NVIDIA unveiled DLSS 4.5 Ray Reconstruction, a second-generation transformer model that promises to improve ray traced and path traced image quality across supported PC games.

DLSS 4.5 Ray Reconstruction will be available in August with several improvements over the first-generation that launched in August 2023:

  • Efficient Denoiser: The new model delivers 35% more compute capability and processes 20% more parameters while maintaining similar performance to the previous model.
  • Enhanced Super Resolution: Building on the advances from DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution, the model has deeper spatial awareness across every scene and more intelligently uses game-engine pixel sampling and motion data. The result is improved lighting accuracy, better temporal stability, and clearer motion in ray-traced and path-traced content.
  • Expanded Training Dataset: Trained on a larger dataset, the new model is even better at image reconstruction. This intelligence gives the model even better awareness to pick the most accurate engine data to reconstruct scenes closer to ground truth.
  • Finer Developer Control: The new model offers developers greater control over temporal accumulation, enabling precise tuning of model response for even better image quality.

NVIDIA demonstrated specific instances of games benefiting from DLSS 4.5 Ray Reconstruction. For example, Indiana Jones and the Great Circle by MachineGames resolves snow much better; PRAGMATA by CAPCOM features fewer residual artifacts and gets improved lighting responsiveness; Alan Wake 2's famous CRT television scene now properly shows the individual lines of static noise.

Upon launching in August, GeForce RTX users will be able to inject the new DLSS 4.5 Ray Reconstruction into 27 games:

NVIDIA also took the opportunity to share some additional DLSS news.

  • The early access co-op survival horror game Backrooms: Escape Together has just been updated to support DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution and Dynamic Multi Frame Generation.
  • Duet Night Abyss adds native support for DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution and Dynamic Multi Frame Generation with an update due on June 2.
  • NARAKA: BLADEPOINT will upgrade to native DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution with an update launching on June 5.
  • Marvel Rivals will upgrade to native DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution and Dynamic Multi Frame Generation with an update due on June 12.
  • The tactical FPS Squad is updating to native DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution and Dynamic Multi Frame Generation with an update scheduled for June 15.
  • Where Winds Meet will get native DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution and Dynamic Multi Frame Generation "soon".
  • The Gothic Remake launches on June 5 with DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution and Dynamic Multi Frame Generation.
  • Hell Let Loose: Vietnam debuts on June 15 with DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution.
  • The upcoming survival sandbox game Honeycomb: The World Beyond will be released this year with DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution, Dynamic Multi Frame Generation, DLSS Ray Reconstruction, and RTXDI (RTX Direct Illumination).
  • Finally, the MMO shooter Cinder City will debut with DLSS 4.5 Super Resolution, Dynamic Multi Frame Generation, and enhanced ray tracing.

But the elephant in the room among all these Computex 2026 announcements was, undoubtedly, the absence of DLSS 5. Ever since NVIDIA announced the technology at GTC 2026, there has been a very mixed reaction, with many gamers and developers expressing heavy skepticism and disappointment over the AI-powered visual changes, while others embraced the new tech.

The question on everyone's mind right now is: Did NVIDIA cancel it outright? Is it being reworked and reintroduced later? Nobody knows right now, though we'll keep asking NVIDIA in the hope of an answer.

About the author: With over two decades of experience in gaming journalism, Alessio Palumbo has led the gaming vertical at Wccftech since August 2015. He started working at a young age for Italian websites like Everyeye.it, Gamestar.it, Nextgame.it, and Multiplayer.it before kickstarting the indie English-language publication Worlds Factory as its founder and Editor in Chief. In the last decade, he has coordinated the overall output of Wccftech's gaming section, managed PR relations, assigned reviews, produced daily news coverage, edited gaming content as needed, and delivered game reviews. Arguably, his trademark content is the long series of exclusive developer interviews that have been cited by Wikipedia and by the biggest news media and gaming publications. His passion for technology also makes him knowledgeable when it comes to gaming hardware and tech. His favorite genres include RPGs, MMORPGs, and action/adventure games.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.

item.title: Today’s Contexto: Hints & Answer For June 1, 2026
item.content:encoded:
Contexto is a word association game that gives you a new word to guess every single day. With every attempt, the AI algorithm determines how close your guess is to the daily answer based on thematic and contextual similarity.

Sometimes, though, no matter how experienced you are, the word you’re looking for can be incredibly vague and hard to deduce. Luckily for you, this guide is full of hints to give you a helping hand, but make sure you give today’s Contexto a go if you haven’t already!

How To Play Contexto

Contexto is incredibly simple to play, but difficult to master. All you have to do is type words into the search bar, and the AI algorithm will tell you how close your word is to the answer.

You’ll know how close your guess is based on the colour of the bar and the number next to it.

  • Red includes guesses with the number 1,501 and above, meaning you’re way off from the secret word.
  • Orange includes guesses between 301 and 1,500, meaning you’re getting closer, but still some ways off.
  • Green includes guesses between 1 and 300, meaning you’re on the right track.

Best Contexto Tips & Tricks

  • Start off using broad terms, then narrow down once you’ve got under the 1,000 mark.
  • Try using Nouns first, and dip into verbs or adjectives once you’ve discovered the context.
  • Experiment with random words at times, especially as different forms of a root word can rank differently, such as work and worker.
  • Guess away, as unlike other word games like Wordle, you aren’t limited to a strict number of attempts.

Best Starting Words For Contexto

Here are some great starting words to get you going with Contexto:

  • Thing
  • Person
  • Place
  • Animal
  • Work
  • Nature
  • Game
  • Concept
  • Idea
  • Food

Contexto Hints For Today, June 1, 2026

Without giving away today’s Contexto answer, here are a few helpful hints to get you thinking in the right direction:

  • Hint 1: Gemstone
  • Hint 2: Variety of quartz
  • Hint 3: Purple in color

If they don’t help, then here are the nine closest words, going from tenth to second:

  • Chalcedony
  • Facet
  • Sapphire
  • Gemstone
  • Garnet
  • Topaz
  • Aquamarine
  • Peridot
  • Citrine

Today’s Contexto Answer For June 1, 2026

If you’ve tried everything and still can’t figure out today’s Contexto answer, then you can find it down below:

Answer: Amethyst

Amethyst is a purple variety of quartz that is often used in jewellery.

When Is The Next Contexto Game?

Contexto is updated daily at 12 AM or Midnight in your respective time zone, meaning the next game will start on June 2.

The Contexto schedule is very consistent, with a new game releasing at the same time every day.

How did you find today’s Contexto? Share your thoughts in the official Insider Gaming Discord Server.

item.title: RAGNAROK Goes Single-Player for the First Time in 25 Years as Gravity Targets PS5, Xbox, and Switch 2 in 2027
item.content:encoded:
This morning, South Korean developer Gravity announced the RAGNAROK Console Project, a new single player roleplaying game based on the popular RAGNAROK ONLINE franchise.

As suggested by its title, it will be the first installment to launch on consoles (Sony's PlayStation 5, Microsoft's Xbox Series S and X, and the Nintendo Switch 2), though it's also already listed for PC via Steam simply as RAGNAROK PROJECT. The game is targeting a debut in the first half of 2027; development is handled by another Korean studio, Waycoder.

The Steam page includes a detailed overview and PC system requirements:

Set in a familiar yet completely reimagined world, players will confront a collapsing reality and the hidden truths buried within it. Choices and consequences. A journey from which there is no turning back.

A Narrative Built on Choice and Responsibility

Experience a story where beliefs and decisions collide — not a simple tale of good versus evil.

Explore a World on the Brink of Collapse

Travel through deserts, ruins, research facilities, and dimensional rift zones as you uncover the truth behind a world falling apart.

Diverse Characters and Classes

Continue your journey alongside companions with distinct roles and combat styles, including Knights, Mages, Priests, Hunters, and more.

Dimensions and Rifts

Dimensional distortions and the secrets surrounding the Heart of Ymir begin to slowly consume the world.

For those unfamiliar with RAGNAROK ONLINE, it is a long-running Korean MMORPG franchise that started in 2002. It was built around Norse mythology, being inspired by the manhwa Ragnarok by Lee Myung-jin, but with a very distinctive anime-style presentation: 2D character sprites moving in 3D environments. Players create characters, choose from a broad class system, level up through combat and quests, and specialize through job changes and later rebirth/transcendence systems.

A sequel was released in 2012, and a third game is currently in development, with the first developer diary published last week.

About the author: With over two decades of experience in gaming journalism, Alessio Palumbo has led the gaming vertical at Wccftech since August 2015. He started working at a young age for Italian websites like Everyeye.it, Gamestar.it, Nextgame.it, and Multiplayer.it before kickstarting the indie English-language publication Worlds Factory as its founder and Editor in Chief. In the last decade, he has coordinated the overall output of Wccftech's gaming section, managed PR relations, assigned reviews, produced daily news coverage, edited gaming content as needed, and delivered game reviews. Arguably, his trademark content is the long series of exclusive developer interviews that have been cited by Wikipedia and by the biggest news media and gaming publications. His passion for technology also makes him knowledgeable when it comes to gaming hardware and tech. His favorite genres include RPGs, MMORPGs, and action/adventure games.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/ragnarok-single-player-rpg-ps5-xbox-switch-2-2027/

item.title: Model Debut4 coming to Switch 2, PC on August 27
item.content:encoded:
• PC
  • Switch 2

Currently available for Switch.

Publisher FuRyu and developer Racjin will release magazine model and production simulation game Model Debut4 for Switch 2 and PC via Steam on August 27, the companies announced.

Model Debut4 first launched for Switch on November 20, 2025 in Japan, followed by December 10, 2025 worldwide.

In Japan, the Switch 2 version will be available both physically and digitally, and the physical edition will ship on a game-key card. Pre-orders are available now at retailers like Amazon Japan.

FuRyu’s announcement makes no mention of an upgrade path for existing Switch owners.

Here is an overview of the game, via its Steam page:

About

The latest entry in the fashion game series, packed with tons of the newest trend items!

Enjoy fashion, work, romance, and more as you aim to become the No.1 model, the “Top of nicola”!

Story

You are a rookie model aiming to become the No.1 model, the “Top of nicola,” at the nicola Show, a special runway event held once a year!

Take on dream jobs such as magazine modeling, TV appearances, and brand image modeling, and grow as a model while expressing your own unique sense of style.

You can also produce two fellow models and help your small talent agency shine!

Fashion, work, romance, friendship—sparkling days full of fun are waiting for you!

Key Features

  • Trendy Items and Detailed Hair & Makeup Customization – All fashion items are supervised by nicola, a hugely popular magazine among teens in Japan! From the latest trend items to princess styles, Harajuku-inspired looks, and more, there are tons of outfits to enjoy. Freely adjust clothing and hair colors, the placement and size of facial features, makeup intensity, and more to create your perfect look!
  • Take on Dream Jobs and Aim to Become a Top Model – Magazine jobs let you experience everything from coordinating outfits based on a theme to photo shoots and even article layout design! As your model rank rises, you can take on special jobs such as appearing in dramas and variety shows, where you can shoot from various angles in 3D backgrounds. Aim to become the No.1 model, the “Top of nicola”!
  • Produce Your Fellow Models and Grow Your Agency – Style and produce the models in your agency to help it grow! As everyone improves, your agency level will rise. Aim to become the No.1 agency!
  • Go Out with Boys and Fellow Models – Work together, go out, and become closer with boys and fellow models! Capture fun memories by taking videos and photos together, then share them on social media. You can even style the boys and your fellow models!

item.title: Tekken 8 game director Kohei Ikeda leaves Bandai Namco after 20 years
item.content:encoded:
• PC
  • PS5
  • Xbox Series

Ikeda to "continue taking on new challenges as a game developer."

Tekken 8 chief producer and game director Kohei Ikeda has left Bandai Namco after 20 years, he announced.

Ikeda was also the game director of Tekken 7, producer of Tekken 3D: Prime Edition, a game designer on Tekken Tag Tournament 2, and a battle effect director and game designer on Soulcalibur IV.

In his departure message, Ikeda said he is “proud that [he] had the opportunity to create games alongside such incredible people who poured their hearts into this work,” and will “continue taking on new challenges as a game developer.”

Ikeda’s departure follows the departure of another Tekken series veteran, Katsuhiro Harada, who left Bandai Namco after more than 31 years back in December 2025, and recently announced the establishment of VS Studio under SNK.

Read the full message from Ikeda below.

I have officially left Bandai Namco Entertainment after many years with the company.

Ever since I first discovered fighting games as a student, I spent countless days in arcades, completely immersed in competition and battling against others. For someone like me, becoming part of the company I had always admired Namco was truly a dream come true. Looking back now, I realize that the opportunity that led me there was made possible by the many friendships and connections I built through fighting games and the people I met along the way.

As I reflect on the past 20 years at the company, I remember first stepping into game development through Soulcalibur IV. Later, I was given the opportunity to work on the Tekken series—a franchise that had a tremendous impact on my life and one that I had always hoped to someday give back to. Being surrounded by passionate and incredibly talented mentors and colleagues, and dedicating ourselves wholeheartedly to game development together, remains one of the most cherished experiences of my life.

In particular, being entrusted with the role of game director for Tekken 7 and Tekken 8 gave me the opportunity to connect with fans and members of the fighting game community (FGC) from all around the world. Sharing excitement, celebrating together, and spending unforgettable moments with all of you became one of the greatest treasures of my life. There were many joyful moments, and many difficult ones as well. There were times when we celebrated together, and times when I received passionate criticism and encouragement. Yet every one of those moments helped push both myself and the Tekken Project forward.

Whether it was hearing someone at an event say, “Thank you for working so hard for us!” or being told, “You need to balance the game better!”—every one of those comments supported and motivated me. For that, I am truly grateful.

And to the development team members who fought alongside me for so many years I can never thank you enough. There is no doubt that everything we achieved was possible because of this team. I am deeply proud that I had the opportunity to create games alongside such incredible people who poured their hearts into this work.

While my role leading the team as game director is coming to a close, the values that both I personally and the Tekken Project have always cherished staying close to the fans and the community and building the game together with everyone will never change. I have entrusted those values, and that baton, to the incredible team that will continue shaping the future of Tekken. As I look forward to the future of Tekken and the FGC, I will also continue taking on new challenges as a game developer.

I hope our paths cross again someday.

GET READY FOR THE NEXT BATTLE!!!

To everyone who has supported me throughout this journey, thank you from the bottom of my heart.

“With gratitude to Tekken fans and the fighting game community all around the world.”

—Tekken Project Game Director, Kohei Ikeda (Nakatsu)



https://www.gematsu.com/2026/06/tekken-8-game-director-kohei-ikeda-leaves-bandai-namco-after-20-years

Publishers Gravity and Daweon Game Media Lab, and developer Waycoder have announced Ragnarok Console Project for PlayStation 5, Xbox Series, Switch 2, and PC (Steam). It will launch in the first half of 2027.

Ragnarok Console Project is a new game based on the Ragnarok franchise that began with the massively multiplayer online RPG Ragnarok Online.

Here is an overview of the game, via its Steam page:

About

Ragnarok Console Project is a new RPG based on the world of Ragnarok, a legendary intellectual property enjoyed by over 100 million players worldwide.

Set in a familiar yet completely reimagined world, players will confront a collapsing reality and the hidden truths buried within it.

Choices and consequences. A journey from which there is no turning back.

Currently in development, with a planned release in the first half of 2027.

Prologue

When he came to, the first thing that came to mind was not the pain.

“Where is the item?”

It was a question he already knew the answer to.

Gone. An item that, by itself… could shake an entire nation.

At that moment, the mission had not ended. It had begun.

“Failure isn’t the end. In most cases… it’s closer to the beginning.”

Key Features

  • A Narrative Built on Choice and Responsibility – Experience a story where beliefs and decisions collide—not a simple tale of good versus evil.
  • Explore a World on the Brink of Collapse – Travel through deserts, ruins, research facilities, and dimensional rift zones as you uncover the truth behind a world falling apart.
  • Diverse Characters and Classes – Continue your journey alongside companions with distinct roles and combat styles, including Knights, Mages, Priests, Hunters, and more.
  • Dimensions and Rifts – Dimensional distortions and the secrets surrounding the Heart of Ymir begin to slowly consume the world.

Watch the announcement teaser trailer below.

item.title: AMD’s new pitch: our old tech is so good you should just keep using it
item.content:encoded:
Computex 2026 is underway in Taiwan, and we’re expecting all manner of flashy computers with jaw-dropping prices (or no prices at all) as the entire industry navigates RAMageddon.

But for desktop PC gamers, AMD has a different pitch. It’s relaunching three old components alongside a big new promise: you won’t need to buy a new motherboard until 2030.

Today, AMD is promising it will keep supporting its AM5 desktop motherboard socket with new Ryzen processors through 2029, which likely means you can keep upgrading to newer CPUs till the end of the decade without changing your board.

Even if you’re still on the older AM4 socket, you may have one last upgrade left: it’s relaunching a “10th Anniversary” edition of the Ryzen 7 5800X3D to celebrate the 10th anniversary of that AM4 platform. That’ll be $349 on June 25th.

And if you decide now’s the time to switch to AMD or the AM5 socket, the company’s got a new old chip for that too: a $330 Ryzen 7 7700X3D, likely a binned version of the existing 7800X3D. The beefier chip costs $380 to $450, though it can occasionally be found at $320. On paper, the 7700X3D looks only slightly slower:

Again, this is old tech: the 7800X3D came out in 2023; its 9000-series successor came out in late 2024. But the old chip was plenty powerful, as you can see in my colleague Tom Warren’s comparison review with the 9950X3D, 9800X3D, and 7800X3D from 2025. (Check out how much less power the 7800X3D used in his chart, too.)

Meanwhile, in the GPU realm, AMD is finally bringing its formerly China-exclusive Radeon RX 9070 GRE to other countries, including the US, starting June 1st for $549.

That’s not quite as friendly for PC gamers to hear, as $549 was supposed to be the starting price for the notably more powerful RX 9070, not the cut-down GRE version, which trails the RTX 5070.

But the RX 9070 was never broadly available at $549, despite what AMD claimed to us at the time — it launched amidst a GPU shortage, hit $549 once, but the price settled closer to $599 or $620 once that shortage ended. So this would be a bit cheaper.

AMD’s making an interesting pitch at a time that everything, especially gaming, is beginning to feel too expensive. Does it convince you?

Follow topics and authors from this story to see more like this in your personalized homepage feed and to receive email updates.

  • Sean Hollister


https://www.theverge.com/tech/940524/amd-computex-am5-promise-2029-rx9070gre-7700x3d-5800x3d

item.title: The QD-OLED gaming monitor that started it all got a big upgrade
item.content:encoded:
Alienware is taking to this year’s Computex 2026 in Taipei to announce some cool gaming monitors, most notably two exciting OLED options that are coming at different points this year. First off, the company is debuting the successor to its very first QD-OLED gaming monitor from 2022 with a refreshed design and high-end specs that’s coming in July (price hasn’t yet been shared).

The AW3426DW is a 34-inch curved 3,440 x 1,440 resolution with an 1800R curve, just like its predecessor. But the new model has the latest QD-OLED Penta Tandem screen tech that boosts the peak brightness to 1,300 nits from 1,000 nit in the previous version. Its refresh rate has been boosted from 240Hz to 280Hz.

Part of the five-layer Penta Tandem tech is an anti-reflective screen coating that supposedly cuts down glare and boosts deep blacks even in well-lit rooms. That’s great since black sometimes appears purple-ish on previous QD-OLED generations. Something that hasn’t changed model-to-model is pixel density; this one’s still at 110 pixels per inch, which is a bit low for this size (many 27-inch OLED monitors have a similar pixel density).

Alienware’s flagship monitor of the show is a 39-inch 5K curved ultrawide monitor coming this fall that uses LG Display’s fourth-generation Primary RGB Tandem screen tech. The AW3926QW is slightly curvier than the QD-OLED model mentioned above, measuring at 1500R, making it more immersive. It can show a peak brightness of 1,300 nits while displaying content at up to 5K at a 165Hz refresh rate, with a pixel density of 143 pixels per inch. It offers a dual mode to knock the resolution down to 1080p, bumping up the refresh rate to 330Hz with support for custom resolutions and aspect ratios.

The two OLED models have some traits in common with each other, despite using different screen technology. For one, both use RGB stripe subpixels to sharpen text and other details. Color-fringed text has been an issue for some people with previous generations of QD-OLED panels, but not as much with OLEDs. Both have VESA DisplayHDR True Black 500 certification, as well as Dolby Vision HDR support, and both support AMD FreeSync Premium Pro and Nvidia G-Sync. Also, they’re protected by Alienware’s great three-year warranty that covers manufacturer defects along with burn-in coverage, plus enhanced pixel shifting and refreshing algorithms to extend their lifespan.

Despite the similarities, the 5K monitor simply offers more features. It has a KVM switch to let you control multiple PCs through the monitor with a single keyboard and mouse, plus a USB-C video port with support for 90W of PD passthrough power. Beyond that, it has two HDMI 2.1 ports with fixed rate link (FRL) to let PCs display its full resolution and refresh rate, one of which supports eARC and ARC. It also boasts a single DisplayPort 2.1 port with fast UHBR20 bandwidth. The 34-inch model has two HDMI 2.1 FRL ports and a DisplayPort 1.4 port. No price has been announced for the 5K display, but these features won’t come cheap.

Finally, Alienware has two VA LCD gaming monitors coming in July for well under $500. The $399.99 34-inch ultrawide AW3426DWM and the $299.99 flat AW3226DM have respectable specs. Both go up to 240Hz refresh rate with 1-millisecond response time and display 1440p resolution. They feature similar design language to Alienware’s higher-end options, and they support Dolby Vision HDR.

It’s nice to have affordable options, though remember that Alienware’s entry-level 27-inch QD-OLED monitor is $399.99, and by nature of its screen tech, offers much better contrast and color accuracy than either of these upcoming models. It’s a stellar value for the price, as I discovered when I put my own money down on one.

Follow topics and authors from this story to see more like this in your personalized homepage feed and to receive email updates.

  • Cameron Faulkner


https://www.theverge.com/games/938956/alienware-computex-tandem-qd-oled-penta-rgb-stripe-gaming-monitors-specs

item.title: Product Spotlight: Arkham Horror The Card Game Accessories
item.content:encoded:
There's hardly a sound save for the thump, thump of the beating of your own heart. Before you unfurls a horrific monstrosity. You and your fellow investigators have put together the clues, you've got the gear, but can you truly face an Eldritch abomination... without a fancy playmat or deck box? THESE are the questions you must ask yourself as you check out our brand new product spotlight of a suite of incredible Arkham Horror The Card Game Accessories, created (and provided to us by) Gamegenic.

I've spoken about my love of Gamegenic products in the past, they're my go-to storage solution for Magic: the Gathering, and I think the quality across the board is top of class. So when I heard about a new line products specifically designed for Arkham Horror The Card Game I was immediately intrigued. Let's dive in!

Arkham Horror Game Night Chest

First up is this big beauty, the Game Night Chest. Designed to hold just about everything you need in the game in one handy box, I really love the look, feel, and use of magnets to snap the lid snugly into place. There's space for four Investigator Deck Tome boxes (more on those in a bit), as well as a pull out container for all of your tokens.

It's smart storage solutions for a game with SO many bits, pieces, and bobs, plus cards upon cards. If you're seriously into the game, with your same characters growing and moving from campaign to campaign, this could be right for you. It's not cheap, coming in at MSRP $119.99, but if you're really plugged into the game, this is a splashy, premium way to show it off and keep it safe and organized.

Arkham Horror Playmats

Next I want to show off three very cool, very different kinds of playmats available for the game.

First up are these gorgeous full-art Game Mats for the game, including a Shiny playmat (the one in the center of the above pic). These are generic, so if you're just a fan of the artwork and want to swag it with other TCG's, you totally can. But if you're looking for something a little more specific and helpful to your game, Gamegenic's got that too.

This is the Player Mat, and it has a tidy spot for each of your card types as you play the game. I'm a sucker for a playmat like this, though I recognize it's not strictly necessary to play the game. 

You and your fellow investigators may not each feel comfortable shelling out for these, though I will say the MSRP of $16.99 is way more affordable than I would have expected for the quality level on display here. It's smaller than the game mats, which is actually nice if you're all looking to fit one of these on the table.

And speaking of the table, the game itself loves to spread out as you explore more locations, so something like this Center Game Mat can be extremely useful. It takes up the majority of the table at almost 28" x 28", but this will give you the space you need to lay out all the various cards you'll unfurl in any given Act of the game.

Arkham Horror Premium Chaos Tokens & Bag

Here's another item I won't describe as strictly necessary, but if I really love a game the way I'm falling in love with Arkham Horror: The Card Game, and there's an option to swap out cardboard components for fancier ones... I'm definitely taking that option. These tokens replace the chaos tokens and bag included in the game (which, if you haven't played, is sort of how you get random results instead of rolling dice, etc.), and the bag you pull them from, and they have a great feel to them.

The tokens themselves are acrylic, and slide on the table and game mats so nicely. The bag itself is a nice upgrade too, designed to sit flat with some inlaid support on the bottom of the bag.

Arkham Horror Investigator Deck Tome

Saving the best for last in this showcase, let's check out the newly updated entry in this roundup of Arkham Horror The Card Game Accessories, the new Investigator Deck Tomes. Designed to hold your Investigator Deck and keep it organized, I'm most surprised and delighted by these.

There's a deck box designed for each of the different investigator classes, Guardian, Seeker, Rogue, Mystic, Survivor, and a neutral one as well if you prefer that. Using the same magnet tech to keep things snug and locked in place, these boxes keep a full deck safe - even sleeved, and there's a cutout window so you can see your investigator's picture card (as well as their name as it peeks through from their full character card). Each one has an MSRP of $24.99, and though I wouldn't have thought I needed one, now that I've been playing with one I don't think I would go back.

There are more new products we didn't cover, like sleeves and connector tokens, that you should definitely explore. Head over to Gamegenic to check them out! Overall, I think these all range from "nice to have" to "need to have," depending on your level of commitment to the game. If Arkham Horror: The Card Game is the kind of game you break out once in a blue moon, sure - you probably don't need any of this stuff. But if you've got a regular group playing a campaign, I think it's definitely worth it to grab some of the storage solutions and a few Player Mats at the least.

What do you think? Will you be adding any of these to your collection? Let us know in the comments below!

The products used in the creation of this feature were provided by Gamegenic and Asmodee USA.

Giaco Furino joined the TechRaptor team as a Staff Writer in 2019 after searching for a dedicated place to write and talk about Tabletop Games. In 2020, he… More about Giaco

item.title: NYT Strands #819 Hints and Answers for Today, May 31, 2026
item.content:encoded:
Strands is a daily word search puzzle game from The New York Times. It is a relatively new game, launched in 2024, in which players seek a common theme among words in a 6×8 letter grid.

Whether it’s your first time playing or you’re a Strands veteran, today’s tips and strategies can help you find words faster. So, if you’ve found yourself stuck with today’s Strands, don’t worry; below you’ll find some helpful hints and even the answers for Strands Game #819 for Sunday, May 31, 2026

To play today’s Strands, head to the game’s website.

IMPORTANT: The following information includes spoilers on today’s edition of Strands. If you don’t want the answer given to you, stop scrolling.

How To Play Strands

Strands is a daily word search that challenges you to find words with a common theme. There’s also a Spanagram that reveals the deeper theme behind the words.

  • Words that are three letters or fewer do not count.
  • Words that reveal the correct theme appear in blue.
  • The Spanagram turns yellow when found.
  • Finding three words that don’t fit the theme will reveal a hint to help you find the proper words.

The goal is to string together as many words as possible. Every letter on the grid gets used! Daily puzzles keep players coming back for more, making it a fun brain exercise and a popular online trend.

Strands can only be played on the New York Times website or on the NYT Games app.

Strands Hints for Today, May 31, 2026

The Strands’ answers to the theme of Game #819 are all terms related to what someone may find themselves doing as a tourist on vacation.

General Strands Hints for May 31

Without giving today’s Strands answers completely away, here are a few helpful hints for today to get you started:

  • Hint 1: Super, Grocery, Flea.
  • Hint 2: Ben Stiller spent many nights at these.
  • Hint 3: Sorry, Mario. You’re princess in another one.

Use these clues to narrow down your searches and increase your chances of solving Strands #819 for May 31, 2026.

Words to Help You Find Strand Hints for May 31, 2026

If you want help getting started without having today’s Strands answer given to you, there are a few starter words worth trying that will help you unlock some in-game hints. A few starter words that may help are:

  • ROAM
  • CAST
  • STARE
  • STALE
  • STAT

Today’s Strands Answer for Game #819 on May 31, 2026

If you’ve tried everything, including using the above hints, and are still stuck, you can find the answers to today’s Strands below. Check out the answers for past puzzles here.

  • BEACH
  • MUSEUM
  • RESTAURANT
  • MARKET
  • CASTLE
  • MONUMENT
  • SPANAGRAM: TOURISM

If you solved today’s Strands without our answers, congratulations! If not, take note of the best tips above for tomorrow’s puzzle, and check back daily for more hints.

When Is The Next Strands Game After #819?

All New York Times games are updated daily at 12 am or Midnight in your respective time zone. Information about the next game is often available in the hours leading up to it, giving players a heads-up. The game on June 1 will be Strands Game #820.

What Is Strands?

Strands is a daily word search puzzle game created by The New York Times. Launched in 2024, Strands challenges players to discover a common theme among words in a 6×8 letter grid. The game’s original concept was developed by Juliette Seive, a research director on The New York Times Games team, with the aim of replicating Wordle’s success. Tracy Bennett has served as the puzzle editor for Strands, overseeing the creation and curation of daily puzzles since the publication’s launch.

For even more Insider Gaming delivered straight to your inbox, sign up for our weekly newsletter.

MOBILE APP

Level Up Your Gaming News

Real-time news, exclusive podcasts, push alerts, and a better reading experience.

Available on iOS & Android

item.title: Former BioWare Veteran Warns AAA Gaming Is Too Dependent on Live-Service Titles
item.content:encoded:
Mark Darrah has producer credits on games in major franchises, including Anthem, Mass Effect, and Dragon Age. Recently, he offered comments on the video game industry’s business model and what it can learn from the movie business.

Darrah noted that the games business is missing the live theater experience that movies still benefit from. He also commented on the subscription model, suggesting that more games should regularly leave subscription platforms, just like shows and movies on Netflix and other streaming platforms. He also suggested that if an alternative to the live service model isn’t found soon, many top-level games may not exist without it.

Mark Darrah’s Quote on the Live-Service Model in Gaming

“I think that the overreliance on microtransactions is overemphasizing certain genres and preventing other genres from flourishing,” he said in a YouTube video. “So, is it worth a think? I would say that it is. Do I have a great answer? Do I have a great model? I don’t. Not yet.

“But it’s something I think that the industry should be considering because everything can’t be a live service, as we’ve, I hope, proven pretty definitively over the last year and a half. If our monetization is coming primarily from live services, we run the risk of ending up in a world where there are no AAA games that aren’t live services. I don’t think that’s a world that any of us really want to live in.”

In 2026, the games industry has seen a number of layoffs and project shutdowns, many of them tied to live-service games. Epic Games recently laid off over 1,000 people and shut down several Fortnite modes. Learn more about that here. Also, Highguard went from being the final game featured at the 2025 Game Awards to launching and then shutting down in just 3 months. Learn more about that here.

What do you think about the comments made by Mark Darrah? Sound off about this story and all others on our official Discord server. Also, don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: Here’s your first look at ‘A Minecraft Movie Squared’ with Kirsten Dunst as Alex
item.content:encoded:
Terrence O'Brien

is the Verge’s weekend editor. He has over 18 years of experience, including 10 years as managing editor at Engadget.

The A Minecraft Movie sequel officially has a title: A Minecraft Movie Squared. What’s more, we now know that Kirsten Dunst will star as Alex, the game’s female character option, and that Matt Berry is set to play an even bigger role in this film. He voiced Nitwit in the first movie, but in this one, he’ll be playing a human character. Who exactly he’ll be hasn’t been revealed yet, but in the clip below, he can be heard dismissing a costume choice because “Steve would not be wearing that,” suggesting he might be playing the Herobrine, a creepy pasta evil counterpart to Jack Black’s Steve.

The director, Jared Hess, and Mojang Chief Creative Officer, Jens Bergensten, also took the opportunity during the May Minecraft Live event to announce the Minecraft Movie Build Challenge. The contest will see players submit their most epic Minecraft creations, with the winning build being featured during the end credits. The full details for the contest can be found here. But you’ll have to wait until A Minecraft Movie Squared release date of July 23th, 2027 to see the winner on the big screen.

Follow topics and authors from this story to see more like this in your personalized homepage feed and to receive email updates.

  • Terrence O'Brien

item.title: Call of Duty Promises No BS or Silly Skins in Modern Warfare 4
item.content:encoded:
Call of Duty says there will be no BS and no silly skins in the upcoming Modern Warfare 4 video game.

With the popularity of games like Fortnite, which enable cartoony crossovers and outlandish skins, Call of Duty has followed suit. Partnerships with IPs such as WWE and Beavis and Butthead have brought similar silliness at the expense of the tense nature of CoD stories and gameplay. That appears to be changing, however.

Call of Duty recently revealed the trailer for the upcoming Modern Warfare 4, which will release in October 2026. On social media, the team behind the game stated that they’ve heard the feedback and that this next game will feature no BS or ridiculous skins. The experience will be all about staying true to the intense nature of Modern Warfare.

This next Call of Duty is important for multiple reasons. It is the first Call of Duty game on a Nintendo platform in quite a while. Also, it will not be available for PS4 or Xbox One. To that point, support for Call of Duty: Warzone, the Battle Royale mode in the Call of Duty franchise, will end on those platforms as well. Learn more here.

This next Call of Duty game will also not be released on day one on XBOX Game Pass. The XBOX brand is also undergoing many changes that involve listening to its audience. Learn more here.

How do you feel about this declaration from the COD team? Sound off about this story and all others on our official Discord server. Also, don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: NEARstudios Sets Expectations for Hawthorn Release Date News
item.content:encoded:
Heather Cerlan of NEARstudios says more work remains before Hawthorn is ready to reveal a release date and launch plans.

Hawthorn is a very special game. Former developers from Bethesda, BioWare, and Naughty Dog have teamed up to bring players a Sandbox RPG that supports co-op or solo experiences as they build their villages. The game also has personality-rich NPCs. It was one of the many games featured in the May 2026 Insider Gaming Showcase. Now, the studio’s co-founder is offering an update on the status of a potential release date for the anticipated nature-driven sandbox.

NEARstudios Founder Has an Update for Fans Waiting on Hawthorn

As part of the Insider Gaming Showcase, Cerlan was interviewed by our Executive Editor Mike Straw. She explained that fan response to the game and the launch of its Kickstarter campaign served as a eureka moment, proving the team was on to something players would actually be interested in.

She also explained that she’s happy this game could serve as a form of escapism for players who may want to escape the uncertainty and negativity of the current news cycle.

When asked about a potential launch date, Cerlan says that the early access date remains a moving target. Funding remains key, as it allows them to keep pushing the game’s limits further. When they are ready to announce a date, the community will know ASAP.

“For us, it’s a rolling target because we’re just looking to bring in little bits of funding wherever we can so we can keep pushing our early access date out because obviously the more time we have, the better we can make the offering to players as this kind of initial rollout,” she said. “We don’t have any dates to announce, but I’ll just say that we just keep nudging it out as we get a little bit more.

“A little more additional funding here and there, whether we get, like, I mean, we’ve been able to do this through obviously the Kickstarter, but we’ve had a few angel investors that have given us very small checks; we’re talking like $50K or something like that. Just little bits of cash here and there to keep things rolling. So it’s one of those things where we’ve got a few different things we’re looking at, different options that we’re assessing right now. Depending on what choice we make, that would determine when that date will come.”

Insider Gaming will continue to monitor the story and report any updates as more information becomes available. You can Wishlist it and check out the demo on Steam. To check out a comprehensive list on Steam of all the games featured at the Insider Gaming Showcase on May 28, click here.

Are you interested in Hawthorn? Sound off about this story and all others in our official Discord server. Also, don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: Jetpack Joyride Designer’s Upcoming Golf Sim Makes Compelling Case For Miserable Controls
item.content:encoded:
Luke Muscat is the designer behind ultra-hits like Fruit Ninja and Jetpack Joyride. Those games excelled due to their accessible, simplistic control schemes. His upcoming project, Normal Golf Game, is taking a different approach. An absurdist swing at 18 holes, Normal Golf asks you to play an unreal round with all-too-real input considerations.

In the brief demo, you arrive at the local club to discover a six-figure tee fee and also your car has been towed. Asked to take down outdated parking signs, you’ll discover just how steep the course ahead really is. While a conventional golf game breaks down swing strength, curves and stances to power meters and compasses, nothing is taken for granted in Normal Golf.

Your swing is executed with a whip of your mouse (or joystick). The angle of your club is divvied between four separate inputs, and it sways and swerves unsteadily in your hand. This doesn’t even take into account the weather, which you have no control over, or the lack of an overhead map of the hole, which was always a little supernatural to begin with.

It has a charmed, Bennett Foddy-like quality to it. All basic swings are obliterated into a balancing act, stretching dexterity thin like a waiter juggling a dozen hot plates. Checking and gauging each atom of your stance also feels like becoming your own father, paternally calling to yourself to square your shoulders every three seconds. You’ll be lobbing balls at gongs, bowling pins and giant toilets. The compelling thing about this demo is how this ultimately captures the golfing experience a lot more accurately than the discretely controlled feeling of your traditional sim.

A low-poly landscape, pierced by a FMV rendered dude as your avatar, Normal Golf is a clear callback to Microsoft’s original Links series. Those brought the weekend nine into the office cubicle. My favorite of the Links games is the much maligned Links Extreme, which added explosives and haunted swamps. It’s dumb and dated on arrival, which is why critics lambasted it and why I appreciate it more than the others. Normal Golf Game is flipping back to these first pages, before the tiny pencil etched the rest of the book. Positing that a more brutally realistic golfing simulator was always within reach. You just might break your rod in two trying to get out of a bunker, is all.



https://kotaku.com/jetpack-joyride-designers-upcoming-golf-sim-makes-compelling-case-for-miserable-controls-2000700905

item.title: Eggstinct Mixes Rocket League Tricks With Mario 64 Platforming
item.content:encoded:
Eggstinct is one of the many games featured as part of the recent Insider Gaming Showcase. Now, the game’s creator is sharing more about the classic games that influenced the forthcoming title.

On the surface, a game where you play as dinosaurs throwing eggs might seem like any Yoshi title or a Super Smash Bros. experience. However, Jesse Jenkins, the Founder of Manaforce Studios, has drawn inspiration from classic titles from decades past and more modern multiplayer hits to create a floppy dinosaur experience that players will hopefully enjoy with plenty of friends.

Eggstinct Is a Creative Mix of Rocket League and Mario 64

As part of the recent Showcase, Jenkins spoke to Insider Gaming’s Mike Straw and explained that the initial idea for the game stemmed from a desire to play a 1990s 3D platformer in multiplayer. Specifically, Jenkins cited Super Mario 64 and Spyro the Dragon as inspirations.

“The initial idea was we wanted to take classic platforming and make it like multiplayer physics-based. So, imagine Mario 64, Spyro, if you were able to play that with your friends, you know what I mean? That’s where we’ve headed. And you know, with all of that being said, we also want to make sure that we have kind of like a robust environment for cooperative and competitive gameplay. So that was kind of the initial hook. Okay. Multiplayer physics-based floppy dinosaur game.”

Furthermore, they added elements from Tony Hawk Pro Skater and Rocket League, including scoring mechanics and trick-based bonuses.

“To expand on it, we have created this kind of score system which takes a lot of inspiration from games like Rocket League and Tony Hawk. So, basically, all of the ways that you score in the game is you perform different kinds of tricks, and you launch the egg into the nest, and you get like these trick multipliers based on how you score your egg, right? We’re still in kind of the beginning phases of it, but it’s going to be more complex… also just really trying to focus on making that feel really, really fun and unique every time that you score an egg.”

Eggstinct currently doesn’t have a solidified release date. Players can currently request access to a playtest via Steam. To check out a comprehensive list on Steam of all the games featured at the Insider Gaming Showcase on May 28, click here.

Are you interested in Eggstinct? Sound off about this story and all others in our official Discord server. Also, don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: An Essential Cyberpunk Roguelike Returns In 868-BACK
item.content:encoded:
In 2013, New Zealand indie developer Michael Brough released 868-HACK, a turn-based iPhone game revolving around cyber attacks and difficult circuitry. Inspired by roguelikes before it, Hack, FTL and Spelunky are accredited for kicking off the genre in the indie gaming space. Now the computer commander is back, jacking in to a more accessible port in a tough-as-ever classic.

In 868-BACK, all data is monopolized by a monolithic corporation. Your job is to humble their supposedly impenetrable systems and return cyberspace to the people. Controlling a one-eyed smiley face, you collect siphons, use them to extract resources straight off the circuit board, and dance around the anti-virus programs in a turn-based waltz. It all appears simplistic and controllable, but most of the ‘Broughlikes’ do. At first.

Both 868 games are fiercely difficult. There are only a handful of enemies that move in consistent, predictable patterns, not unlike Pac-Man’s ghostly fiends. Large, open maps give you ample room to siphon up data without triggering any alarms, but provide zero cover when threats emerge. Likewise, narrow spaces provide a more controlled environment, but it’s unlikely you’ll siphon anything without summoning an ambush. Your best resources are “progs,” unique power ups that grant you new ways to attack and navigate the grid. Often the handier the prog, the higher the risk it will be to grab them.

BACK doesn’t fuss with the formula too much. There are new progs, and some revisions, but for returning players it certainly feels like getting back on the bike. New players are the biggest benefactors of the sequel. On top of, I’ll be damned, a tutorial, the game also features an easily accessed index, which will illustrate what each prog and special ability look like in action. The biggest change-up is the structure. While the original was almost arcade-like in its straight forwardness, BACK sees you surfing between different servers with individual conditions. How you leap between them, or where you run to when your tail’s between your legs, adds to the depth of your assault on the system.

The game also features a snazzy new paint job. While the cyberpunk bonafides were never in question, BACK has a far scuzzier, nastier look. Compared to the more elegant pixel work of the original, these sprites seem much more summoned from some corruptive place. Never fear on the audio front. The vocalized blips and *kshhhs* stuck around.

Intended or not, 868-HACK served as a great entry point for Brough’s body of work. Lovingly known as ‘Broughlikes,’ these super simple, super tough, turn-based games like Cinco Paus and Corrypt have taken on a life of their own. Now BACK serves as an entry point to that original node. A nexus into a mean, rewarding web of thrills.



https://kotaku.com/an-essential-cyberpunk-roguelike-returns-in-868-back-2000700901
